subsections.title: Kafka Core Concepts
subsections.keyRule: Kafka = distributed event streaming platform. Messages called events/records. Stored in Topics. Topics split into Partitions for parallelism. Producers write, Consumers read. Consumer Groups enable parallel consumption. Messages retained for a configurable time — NOT deleted after consumption.

terms.what: A Topic is a named stream of events — like a DB table but for events. Each Topic is split into Partitions — ordered, immutable logs. Partitions enable parallel processing.
terms.why: Without partitions, one consumer reads all events — cannot scale. With 10 partitions and 10 consumers, 10x parallel processing.
terms.how: Topic 'orders' with 10 partitions. Messages with same key (customerId) always go to same partition — preserving order per customer. Each partition has a sequential offset.
terms.where: Decoupled event-driven microservices: order-service publishes 'order-placed', multiple services consume independently.
terms.when: More partitions = more parallelism but more overhead. Rule: number of consumers <= number of partitions. Add partitions only when needed.
terms.issue: Increasing partition count for an existing topic reassigns messages — breaks ordering guarantees for key-based routing.
terms.fix: Plan partition count at topic creation. Start with enough (10-50 for high-volume). Cannot reduce partitions — only increase.

star.s: Interview: how does Kafka scale consumers?
star.a: Each consumer in a group gets exclusive partitions. Add consumers up to partition count = linear scaling. Beyond partition count, extra consumers idle.
star.r: Correct explanation of consumer group scaling.

terms.what: Producer sends (publishes) events to Kafka topics. Can specify: key (determines partition), value (event payload), headers, timestamp.
terms.why: Producers are the source of all events in Kafka. The key determines routing — same key = same partition = ordered delivery for that key.
terms.how: Spring Kafka: @Autowired KafkaTemplate<String,OrderEvent> template; template.send('orders', order.getId().toString(), orderEvent);
terms.where: Any service that needs to publish events: order-service publishes order events, payment-service publishes payment results.
terms.when: Always set a meaningful key for ordering guarantees. Use null key for even round- robin distribution with no ordering needed.
terms.issue: Producer not acknowledging — data loss if broker crashes before writing. Default acks=1 only waits for leader broker.
terms.fix: Set acks=all for critical data — waits for all in-sync replicas to confirm. Set retries=3 and idempotent=true to prevent duplicates on retry.

star.a: Producer had acks=1 (leader only). Changed to acks=all with retries=3 and enable.idempotence=true.
star.r: No message loss. Exactly-once delivery for critical orders.

terms.what: Consumer reads events from Kafka topics. Consumers in the same Consumer Group share partitions — each partition assigned to one consumer. Different groups each get all events independently.
terms.why: Consumer groups enable parallel processing (one group) AND fan-out (multiple groups each process all events — order-service AND inventory-service both get all order events).
terms.how: @KafkaListener(topics='orders', groupId='inventory-service') void processOrder(OrderEvent event) { ... } — inventory-service processes all orders.
terms.where: Every service that reacts to events from another service.
terms.when: One consumer group per downstream service. Each service independently reads at its own pace — Kafka retains messages for both.

terms.what: Offset is the sequential position of each event in a partition. Consumer tracks its offset — knows where to resume if restarted. Retention = how long Kafka keeps messages (default 7 days).
terms.why: Unlike traditional queues, Kafka keeps messages after consumption. Multiple consumers can read the same message. You can replay from any offset.
terms.how: Reset offset to beginning: consumer group --reset-offsets --to-earliest — reprocess all messages. Skip ahead: --to-latest. Specific time: --to-datetime.
terms.where: Every Kafka consumer setup. Offset management is critical for reliability.
terms.when: Reset offsets when: deploying a fix for bad processing, testing with real data, adding a new consumer that needs historical events.
terms.issue: Consumer offset committed too early — consumer restarts and skips messages. Or committed too late — reprocesses messages causing duplicates.
terms.fix: Implement idempotent consumers — if same event processed twice, result is same. Use unique event ID + DB check: 'have I seen this ID?'

star.s: Deployed buggy consumer — 3 hours of events processed incorrectly.
star.a: Reset consumer group offset to 3 hours ago. Redeploy fixed consumer.
star.r: All events reprocessed correctly. Kafka retention made this easy.

terms.what: Spring Kafka's @KafkaListener annotation wires a method to consume from Kafka topics. Handles consumer lifecycle, threading, error handling, and deserialization.
terms.why: Removes boilerplate Kafka consumer setup. Clean annotation-driven programming model.
terms.where: Every Spring microservice that consumes Kafka events.
terms.when: Use @KafkaListener for most consumption. Configure concurrency for parallel partition processing: @KafkaListener(concurrency='3').
terms.issue: @KafkaListener method throws exception — depends on config: might stop the consumer, or skip the message, or retry forever.
terms.fix: Configure DefaultErrorHandler with retry backoff. Send to Dead Letter Topic (DLT) after max retries. Monitor DLT for failed messages.

star.s: One bad message in Kafka stops all processing — consumer stuck retrying forever.
star.a: Added DefaultErrorHandler with 3 retries + exponential backoff. After 3 fails, send to orders-DLT. Alert on DLT activity.
star.r: Bad messages isolated. Processing continues. 17. MODERN JAVA — JAVA 8 TO 21

subsections.title: Java 8 Core Features
subsections.keyRule: Java 8 was the biggest Java update since Java 5. Key: Lambda expressions, Stream API, Optional, Default methods in interfaces, new Date/Time API (java.time), CompletableFuture improvements. These are now baseline — every Java developer must know them.

terms.what: Records are immutable data carriers — automatically get constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString based on their components. Minimal boilerplate.
terms.why: Java DTO/value objects needed 10-20 lines for what should be 1 line. Records make immutable data classes concise.
terms.how: record UserDTO(String name, String email) {} — generates: constructor, name(), email(), equals(), hashCode(), toString(). Immutable — all fields are final.
terms.where: DTOs, API response objects, value objects, response bodies, query results.
terms.when: Use records for simple immutable data containers. Do NOT use for JPA entities (need no-arg constructor and mutable fields). Do NOT use when you need inheritance.
terms.issue: Records cannot extend another class (only implement interfaces). Cannot be mutable — no setters.
terms.fix: For entities, use regular classes with Lombok @Data. Use records only for immutable DTOs.

star.s: DTO class is 25 lines for 4 fields (getters, equals, hashCode, toString, constructor).
star.a: record UserDTO(Long id, String name, String email, String role) {} — 1 line.

terms.what: Sealed classes restrict which classes can extend or implement them. You declare the exact allowed subclasses.
terms.why: Enables exhaustive pattern matching — compiler knows all possible subtypes. Like a controlled enum but for classes.
terms.how: sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {} — only these three can implement Shape. Each can be final, sealed, or non-sealed.
terms.where: Domain modeling where you know all variants: payment types, result types (Success/Failure), event types.
terms.when: Use when you know all subtypes at design time and want the compiler to enforce exhaustive handling.
terms.issue: Without sealed, adding a new subclass breaks exhaustive switch — no compiler warning.

terms.what: Pattern matching for instanceof eliminates the cast after instanceof check. Switch expressions can match on type, value, or condition.
terms.why: Old: if (obj instanceof String) { String s = (String) obj; ... } — redundant cast. New: if (obj instanceof String s) { use s directly }.
terms.how: if (obj instanceof String s && s.length() > 5) { ... }. Switch: return switch(shape) { case Circle c -> Math.PI * c.radius() * c.radius(); case Rectangle r -> r.w() * r.h(); };
terms.where: Type-based dispatch, JSON/XML processing, visitor patterns.
terms.when: Use in any instanceof check in new Java 16+ code. Use switch expressions for multi-way type dispatch.
terms.issue: Old code with explicit casts after instanceof — verbose and fragile if wrong cast.
terms.fix: Migrate instanceof + cast pairs to pattern matching instanceof.

star.s: Process different event types with instanceof chains.
star.a: Changed to switch pattern matching. Each case matches type and binds variable.
star.r: 30 lines → 10 lines. Compiler enforces all types covered (with sealed events).

terms.what: Virtual threads are lightweight threads managed by the JVM — not OS threads. You can create millions of them with tiny memory footprint.
terms.why: Platform threads (OS threads) are expensive — each needs 1MB+ stack. Can't have more than ~10,000. Virtual threads need ~few KB each — millions possible.
terms.how: Thread.ofVirtual().start(() -> handleRequest()). Or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() — creates a new virtual thread per task.
terms.where: High-concurrency servers, each request gets its own virtual thread. I/O-bound workloads where threads spend most time waiting.
terms.when: Use virtual threads for I/O-bound concurrent work (HTTP calls, DB queries, file reads). NOT for CPU-bound tasks — no benefit there.
terms.issue: Synchronized blocks pin virtual threads to platform threads — loses the benefit.
terms.fix: Replace synchronized with ReentrantLock in code that will use virtual threads. Avoid long-running synchronized blocks.

star.s: Tomcat with 200 platform threads cannot handle 10,000 concurrent connections.
star.a: Switched to virtual thread executor. Each connection gets its own virtual thread.
star.r: Handles 100,000 concurrent connections on same machine.

subsections.title: Java 9-17 Key Features
subsections.keyRule: Java 9: Module system (Jigsaw), private interface methods. Java 10: var (local type inference). Java 11: String methods (isBlank, strip, lines), HttpClient. Java 14: Switch expressions, Records (preview). Java 15: Text blocks. Java 17: Sealed classes, LTS release.

terms.what: var lets the compiler infer the type of a local variable from the right-hand side. Less verbose, but type is still static — checked at compile time.
terms.why: Removes redundant type repetition: ArrayList<Map<String, List<User>>> list = new ArrayList<>() → var list = new ArrayList<>(). Cleaner code.
terms.how: var users = new ArrayList<User>(); var result = service.findAll(); var entry = map.entrySet().iterator().next();
terms.where: Local variables where type is obvious from context. NOT allowed: method parameters, return types, fields.

terms.what: Text blocks are multi-line string literals using triple quotes. Preserve formatting, handle indentation, no need for escape characters.
terms.why: Multi-line SQL, JSON, HTML in strings required messy escaping and concatenation. Text blocks make them readable.
terms.how: String sql = '''SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = true ORDER BY name'''; String json = '''{ "name": "Alice", "role": "admin" }''';
terms.where: SQL queries, JSON templates, HTML email templates, YAML snippets in code.
terms.when: Use for any multi-line string content. Text blocks trim common indentation automatically.
terms.issue: Text block trims leading whitespace based on the closing ''' position — indentation can be unexpected.
terms.fix: Position closing ''' at the leftmost indent level you want. Or use stripIndent() for explicit control.

star.s: JPQL query with string concat and escaping is unreadable.

terms.what: Java 11's java.net.http.HttpClient is a modern, non-blocking HTTP client built into the JDK. No external library needed for HTTP calls.
terms.why: Old HttpURLConnection was verbose and low-level. HttpClient supports HTTP/2, async calls, and has a clean API.
terms.how: HttpClient client = HttpClient.newHttpClient(); HttpRequest req = HttpRequest.newBuilder().uri(URI.create(url)).GET().build(); HttpResponse<String> resp = client.send(req, BodyHandlers.ofString());
terms.where: Any Java app making HTTP calls to external APIs without wanting to add libraries.
terms.when: Use for simple HTTP calls in Java 11+. For Spring Boot, use WebClient (reactive) or RestTemplate/RestClient for more integration.
terms.issue: Synchronous client.send() blocks the calling thread — for high-concurrency use client.sendAsync() which returns CompletableFuture.

star.s: Java 8 app using Apache HttpClient (external dep).
star.a: Upgraded to Java 11. Migrated to built-in HttpClient.

subsections.title: Kafka Internals
subsections.keyRule: Broker = Kafka server that stores partitions. Replication factor = copies of each partition across brokers. ISR = in-sync replicas (fully caught up). At-most-once = may lose messages. At-least- once = may duplicate. Exactly-once = no loss, no duplicate (Kafka transactions + idempotent producer). Dead Letter Topic = error queue for failed messages.

terms.what: Broker: a Kafka server that stores and serves partition data. A Kafka cluster has multiple brokers. Replication factor: how many broker copies of each partition exist — for fault tolerance.
terms.why: If one broker goes down, other brokers holding replicas serve traffic. Replication factor 3 = one partition leader + two followers — tolerates 2 broker failures.
terms.how: Each partition has one leader (handles all reads/writes) and N-1 followers (replicate from leader). Replication factor set per topic: kafka-topics.sh --replication-factor 3.
terms.where: Production Kafka clusters. Minimum 3 brokers for replication factor 3. More brokers = more parallelism.
terms.when: Set replication factor based on fault tolerance needs: RF=1 for dev, RF=3 for production. Higher RF = more storage, slightly more latency.
terms.issue: RF=1 means one broker down = topic unavailable. For production, always use RF >= 3.
terms.fix: Create topics with: --replication-factor 3. Configure min.insync.replicas=2 so writes require 2 brokers to confirm.

star.s: Interview: how does Kafka handle broker failure?
star.a: Leader election — a follower in ISR becomes new leader. If RF=3 and min.insync.replicas=2, one broker can fail without data loss.

terms.what: ISR (In-Sync Replicas) is the set of replicas fully caught up with the partition leader. If a replica falls behind, it leaves ISR. Only ISR members can become leader.
terms.why: If a replica is lagging (behind the leader), making it the new leader would lose messages. ISR ensures only up-to-date replicas are candidates for leadership.
terms.how: Producer acks=all: waits for all ISR replicas to acknowledge before confirming. If ISR shrinks to 1 (only leader), acks=all still succeeds (just the leader).
terms.where: Kafka broker replication. Affects: data durability guarantees, producer acknowledgment.
terms.when: Monitor ISR size. If ISR drops below replication factor, a replica is lagging — investigate broker performance.
terms.issue: A lagging replica being out of ISR = if leader fails now, some messages may be in the leader's log but not in any ISR replica — potential data loss.
terms.fix: Monitor ISR with kafka-topics.sh --describe. Alert when under-replicated partitions exist. Tune replica.lag.time.max.ms.

star.a: Slow broker falling behind — leaves ISR. Found disk I/O bottleneck on that broker. Fixed disk.
star.r: Replica catches up, rejoins ISR. Full redundancy restored.

terms.what: At-most-once: commit offset before processing — message may be lost if processor crashes after commit. At-least-once: commit after processing — message processed at least once, may be duplicated on retry. Exactly-once: Kafka transactions + idempotent producer — no loss, no duplicate.
terms.why: Different systems have different tolerance for loss vs duplication. Choose delivery semantics based on business requirements.
terms.how: At-most-once: enable.auto.commit=true, auto.commit.interval.ms=1000. At-least- once: manual commit after processing. Exactly-once: spring-kafka transaction + idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true).
terms.where: At-most-once: metrics (losing a counter is OK). At-least-once: order processing with idempotent handlers. Exactly-once: financial transactions.

terms.what: Dead Letter Topic stores messages that failed processing after max retries. Instead of losing them, Spring Kafka sends them to a DLT for error handling and investigation.
terms.why: Messages fail for many reasons (bad data, downstream unavailable). DLT captures them for analysis and potential reprocessing — no data loss.
terms.how: Spring Kafka auto-configures DLT: @KafkaListener topic fails → retried N times → sent to 'topicName.DLT'. Monitor DLT for processing errors.
terms.where: All production Kafka consumers. DLT is standard practice for reliable message processing.
terms.when: Always configure DLT for production consumers. Process DLT regularly — alert on DLT growth.
terms.issue: Ignoring DLT causes message buildup — data silently lost. DLT itself can fill up if underlying bug not fixed.
terms.fix: Set up DLT consumer to alert on failures. Process DLT: fix bug, replay messages. Use exponential backoff before DLT.

star.s: Kafka consumer occasionally fails on malformed messages — messages silently dropped.
star.a: DLT configured. Failed messages go to orders.DLT. Alert set up. Regular DLT audit.
star.r: No messages lost. All failures visible and actionable.
